one_line_takeaway: TAMI-MPC collapses the interactive-round complexity of MPC secure comparison (Millionaires' protocol) to one round per primitive by deriving correlated randomness from synchronized TEE seeds instead of oblivious transfer, restricting TEE trust to the offline phase, and pairs this with an FPGA accelerator to deliver up to 4.86x (ResNet-50) and 7.44x (BERT-base) end-to-end inference speedups over Cheetah/Bumblebee under constrained mobile-network and IoT-class client conditions.
key_finding: Protocol-level (Table 2): leaf-comparison online communication drops from n(k+2k) bits to nk bits with rounds reduced from 2 to 1; tree-merge online communication drops from 8(n-1)log2(n) bits to (n-1) bits with rounds reduced from log2(n) to 1, and offline ROT cost for both primitives is eliminated (0 offline communication). Randomness-reuse (Opt.#2) yields up to 584x reduction in correlated-randomness generation volume/time over 32-64 bit input lengths (Figure 9). FPGA microbenchmarks (Table 3, vs. Cheetah on an 800 MHz/512 MB CPU): CRH pipeline interleaving gives 3.97x speedup, propagating to 2.72x for the full leaf-comparison unit (FComp); the memory-efficient/packed tree-merge datapath gives 80.15x speedup for FPolyMult; the full Millionaires' protocol (FMill) is 3.38x faster overall, using ~30% BRAM and ~1% DSP on the Z-7030. Nonlinear-layer microbenchmarks (Figure 10, input size 2x10^5): up to 7x speedup over Cheetah's ReLU in the WAN setting, 8.8x over Bumblebee's Softmax, 10x over Bumblebee's GeLU, rising to 17x for GeLU with FPGA acceleration as network quality improves. End-to-end (Table 4): under the mobile network setting, TAMI-MPC completes ResNet-50 inference in 108s (4.86x speedup over Cheetah's 525s) and SqueezeNet in 24s (4.95x over Cheetah's ~64s baseline is 64s->... note Cheetah SqueezeNet Mobile: base 317s, ours 64s, 4.95x); it achieves 7.44x speedup over Bumblebee for BERT-base under mobile (2828s base -> 380s ours). Speedups over CryptFlow2 range 1.88x-4.42x across LAN/WAN/Mobile.
core_contribution: TAMI-MPC is a hardware/protocol co-design for the secure-comparison (Millionaires') protocol used in 2-party MPC-based private inference. Its own contributions (not cited prior work) are: (1) a redesigned TEE-assisted leaf comparison F_Comp that removes the first online OT round by having both parties locally derive the input-independent mask x_j and the OT selection bit c from synchronized seeds inside their TEEs (building on the authors' own prior SilentFlow work [11] for non-interactive ROT derivation, but newly retaining and discarding c/x_j inside the TEE to collapse 2 online rounds to 1); (2) a one-round tree-merge primitive F_PolyMult that replaces Beaver-triple multiplication with a single polynomial-multiplication exchange, collapsing log2(n) online rounds to 1 by rewriting the AND-tree as a masked polynomial expansion whose subset-product randomness is generated non-interactively inside the TEE rather than via ROT; (3) two protocol-level optimizations -- interleaving leaf comparison and tree merge to halve F_PolyMult communication (Opt.#1), and a correlated-randomness reuse scheme based on an inclusion-exclusion counting argument over an exponent matrix that reduces the naive exponential blow-up of subset-product randomness to a near-linear cost (Opt.#2, Eq. 4-7); and (4) a specialized FPGA accelerator (Zynq-7030) with a pipeline-aware interleaved CRH (correlation-robust hash) datapath for leaf comparison and a memory-efficient/packed-polynomial datapath for tree merge.
threat_model: 2-party secure inference (client holds input, server holds model weights) under a semi-honest MPC threat model, matching the security guarantees of the underlying Millionaires' protocol (CryptFlow2-style). The paper's distinguishing security claim (Section 2.2) is about the TEE's role in the trust boundary: unlike prior TEE-based PPML systems [25, 26] that import secret client/server data into the TEE for online computation (so a compromised TEE at inference time leaks secrets), TAMI-MPC restricts the TEE to offline, input-independent tasks only (deriving correlated randomness/masks from synchronized seeds). All online-phase computation is done via plain MPC in the untrusted domain. The authors explicitly argue that because the input-independent values x_j and the selection bit c are discarded from the TEE after computing the masked value tmp, inference security holds via MPC alone even if the TEE is compromised during the online phase -- i.e., TEE compromise is explicitly scoped out of the online attack surface by design, rather than defended against by TEE hardening.
limitations: The paper's own security argument (Section 2.2/3.1) is informal/qualitative -- it asserts that discarding x_j and c from the TEE after computing tmp preserves online-phase security 'even if the TEE is compromised,' but the paper does not provide a formal simulation-based security proof or ideal-functionality argument for this claim within the reviewed text; it is stated as a design property rather than proven. The correlated-randomness reuse technique (Opt.#2) is only illustrated via a toy n=3 example and a general counting formula (Eq. 7), without a full worked security/independence argument for why reused randomness does not leak cross-term correlations at scale. Evaluation baselines (CryptFlow2, Cheetah, Bumblebee, BOLT) are all 2-party semi-honest protocols; no comparison to malicious-secure or 3-party MPC systems is presented. The accelerator is evaluated only on a single low-end FPGA (Zynq-7030) and the LLM baseline server is a specific 64-core AMD CPU, so generality across hardware platforms is not demonstrated. The paper builds directly on the authors' own prior work SilentFlow [11] for non-interactive ROT derivation inside the TEE; TAMI-MPC's leaf-comparison round reduction is an extension of that mechanism rather than a wholly independent construction, and the text does not benchmark against non-TEE, ROT-based accelerators like Ironman [12].

quantitative_results: Leaf comparison online communication: n(k+2k) bits (CryptFlow2/BOLT/Cheetah/Bumblebee) reduced to nk bits (TAMI-MPC), online rounds 2 -> 1 (Table 2)
Tree merge online communication: 8(n-1)log2(n) bits reduced to (n-1) bits, online rounds log2(n) -> 1, offline ROT eliminated (Table 2)
Correlated-randomness generation for tree merge: up to 584x speedup across 32-64 bit input lengths (Figure 9)
CRH module (FPGA vs Cheetah CPU baseline): 3.97x speedup, 716.713ms -> 180.451ms (Table 3)
Full Millionaires' protocol (FMill): 3.38x speedup, 608.271ms -> 179.909ms; ~30% BRAM, ~1% DSP on Zynq Z-7030 (Table 3)

Precomputed LUT for correlated-randomness indexing: 4.89x speedup; prefetch/streaming optimization: additional 5.56x gain; packed polynomial execution (up to 512/n comparisons per transfer): additional 154.84x speedup
Nonlinear layer microbenchmarks (input size 2x10^5): up to 7x over Cheetah ReLU (WAN), 8.8x over Bumblebee Softmax, 10x over Bumblebee GeLU, up to 17x for GeLU with FPGA acceleration (Figure 10)
End-to-end mobile-network inference: ResNet-50 108s (4.86x vs Cheetah 525s); SqueezeNet 24s (4.95x vs Cheetah 317s); BERT-base 380s (7.44x vs Bumblebee 2828s) (Table 4)
End-to-end speedups vs CryptFlow2: SqueezeNet 1.88x(LAN)/3.02x(WAN)/3.98x(Mobile); ResNet-50 2.63x(LAN)/3.54x(WAN)/4.42x(Mobile) (Table 4)

8x reduction in communication cost (bits) claimed for leaf comparison/tree merge combined; interactive rounds reduced from 2 and log2(n) to 1 respectively; TEE-side randomness generation cost reduced by over 79x (Introduction, contributions list)
baselines_compared: SilentFlow (authors' own prior ASP-DAC 2026 work, used as the ROT-generation building block, not an independent external baseline)
